Skip to main content
MOBIUS/v0.1
Simulation How it works CLI Philosophy github

adversarial agent orchestration

MOBIUSThe swarm runs below.

Mobius pits agents from Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI against each other on your tasks. A cross-family judge panel scores every match. Losers get studied and rewritten. The loop repeats until your swarm converges toward your quality bar.

$ pip install mobius-swarm

competition in progress
mobius run "write a one-paragraph poem about light"
mobius — live match

Live competition simulation

What you just watched

The closed loop.

  1. 01 COMPETE All agents receive the same task and execute in parallel. Provider diversity — Claude, Gemini, GPT — means no single family's blind spots dominate.
  2. 02 JUDGE A cross-family panel — Claude Opus, Gemini Pro, GPT-4o — scores every response. No provider holds a majority. Structural bias mitigation, not procedural.
  3. 03 RANK Elo ratings update based on who beat whom. Upsets count. Streaks compound. Your personal leaderboard, shaped by your own tasks.
  4. 04 EVOLVE Losers, not champions. The winner's approach is analyzed; underperformers are rewritten to close the gap. Refining what already works misses the point.
  5. N+ REPEAT Over N rounds, the swarm converges toward your specific quality bar. Not a benchmark committee's definition — yours.

Architecture

Adversarial
by design.

  • Multi-provider swarm

    Agents run on Claude, Gemini, and GPT simultaneously. Provider diversity prevents any single model's blind spots from defining the outcome.

  • Elo rating system

    Borrowed from chess. An agent's rating shifts based on who it beats and who beats it. Upsets count. Streaks compound.

  • Cross-family judges

    Claude + Gemini + GPT-4o judge every match. No family holds majority. Bias is structural, not procedural.

  • Skills as differentiator

    Agents compete with different tool sets. A research agent vs. a code agent on the same task reveals deeper capability gaps.

  • Free Opus orchestration

    Opus judges for free via Claude Code Pro. Haiku and Sonnet run the cheap, parallel swarm via API. API costs only where they matter.

  • Local-first

    SQLite + sqlite-vec. Agent registry and vector memory on disk, no cloud dependency.

$ mobius leaderboard
# Agent Provider Elo W/L Rate
01 razor anthropic 1487 +18 22/5
81%
02 prism-g google 1441 +14 18/9
67%
03 forge openai 1408 +6 14/9
61%
04 echo-v3 anthropic 1372 −24 11/12
48%
05 cobalt google 1338 −16 8/16
33%

CLI

Commands are
the interface.

  • mobius run Start a competition. All agents respond in parallel. Judge panel scores and ranks.
  • mobius evolve Rewrite underperformers based on winner analysis. Losers improve or get replaced.
  • mobius loop Run N rounds of competition and evolution on varied tasks. The self-improvement loop.
  • mobius leaderboard Print Elo rankings with win/loss history and per-provider breakdown.
  • mobius scout Point at a codebase. Auto-generate domain-specific agents tuned to your context.
  • mobius train Iterative training on a single challenge. Refines losers each round until convergence.
  • mobius explain Show the judge panel's full reasoning for the last match. No black box.
bash — getting started

$ pip install mobius-swarm

$ mobius init

$ mobius bootstrap # seed agents via Opus (free)

$ mobius run "your task here"

Runtime

Python 3.12+

Storage

SQLite + sqlite-vec

SDK

claude-agent-sdk

Providers

Anthropic · Google · OpenAI

Philosophy

Why
adversarial?

Pressure reveals capability that benchmarks cannot.

Most AI evaluations are static: run a model against a fixed dataset, compare scores. Benchmarks measure what a model knows the answer to. They don't measure what it does under competition, against capable peers, on tasks that actually matter to you.

Mobius inverts this. It creates a closed loop: compete, judge, evolve, repeat. The task set is yours. The judge panel is cross-family, so no provider wins by default. The evolution loop targets losers, not champions — because refining what's already working misses the point.

The result is a team of agents shaped by your specific domain, your specific quality bar, and continuous adversarial pressure. Over time, the swarm learns what you care about — not what a benchmark committee decided last year.

Because Opus judges for free via Claude Code Pro, and Haiku/Sonnet run the cheap parallel swarm via API, the cost profile actually makes sense. The smart model is free. The fast model is cheap. You're not paying for Opus to do Haiku's work.

Mobius  ·  adversarial agent swarm orchestrator  ·  Python 3.12+, SQLite, claude-agent-sdk
